title: Partner sale never decrements the shared link: syncInventoryChange is called with linkId only and silently ignores it
impact: When a partner sells consigned/shared stock through a real Shopify/Square order webhook (shopify.webhooks.controller.ts:614, square.webhooks.controller.ts:442), the shared link's AvailableQuantity stays stale — it never goes down. The host (and every other partner sharing that source) keeps showing/overselling stock that was already sold. Only the secondary explicit handleCanonicalInventoryUpdate(link.SourceVariantId, sourceUserId, newQuantity) fires, which pushes the computed newQuantity to the SOURCE org's platforms but leaves the canonical link row, partner-side levels, and sibling-partner branches untouched — so the source/partner views diverge and oversell.
evidence: partner-sale.service.ts:118 `await this.crossOrgSharingService.syncInventoryChange({ linkId: link.Id, orgId: partnerOrgId, ... quantityDelta: -quantitySold, reason: 'partner_sale' });` (no variantId). cross-org-inventory-sync.service.ts:142 `.or(\`SourceVariantId.eq.${request.variantId},TargetVariantId.eq.${request.variantId}\`)` and :306 same — both undefined. linkId never referenced anywhere in the implementation.
fix: Either pass variantId in the partner-sale call (e.g. variantId: link.TargetVariantId so isTargetOrg matches and the atomic delta + partner→source propagation runs), or make syncInventoryChange honor request.linkId: when linkId is set, load that single link directly and run the same per-link branch instead of the variant `.or` query. Add a test asserting the atomic RPC + CrossOrgSyncEvents row fire for a partner-sale request that supplies only linkId.
verifier_note: Verified the real implementation: cross-org-sharing.service.ts:156-158 delegates syncInventoryChange straight to CrossOrgInventorySyncService.syncInventoryChange, which keys EVERY link lookup off request.variantId (auto-share existence check at :142, active-links query at :306) and never references request.linkId anywhere. handlePartnerSale (partner-sale.service.ts:118-125) calls it with linkId:link.Id but NO variantId. With variantId undefined, the template `SourceVariantId.eq.${request.variantId},TargetVariantId.eq.${request.variantId}` becomes literal `...eq.undefined`, matching no rows -> links query at :308 returns empty -> early return at :310, so the atomic applyLinkDeltaAtomic RPC never runs, no CrossOrgSyncEvents row is written, no bidirectional partner-level write, and the multi-branch fan-out never fires. Reachable from real Shopify (shopify.webhooks.controller.ts:614) and Square (square.webhooks.controller.ts:442) order webhooks, both of which pass a real ProductVariantId mapping. The only surviving effect is the secondary handleCanonicalInventoryUpdate(link.SourceVariantId, sourceUserId, newQuantity) at :139, which I confirmed (sync-coordinator.service.ts:1408) merely queues a platform push for the SOURCE variant and does NOT call back into syncInventoryChange — so CrossOrgProductLinks.AvailableQuantity is genuinely never decremented and the partner/host views diverge and oversell. No test covers this path (partner-sale.service.spec.ts only tests isPartnererConnection). High severity (oversell + tenant data divergence on a normal reachable path) is correct.
corrected_location: src/organizations/services/partner-sale.service.ts:118-125 (caller); src/organizations/services/cross-org-inventory-sync.service.ts:142 and :306 (variantId-keyed lookups); src/organizations/cross-org-sharing.service.ts:96 + :156-158 (linkId declared but delegate ignores it)

title: Partnership aggregation writes combined source+target total back onto the target's own levels, causing monotonic quantity inflation
impact: Aggregated partnerships drift upward without bound. The partner's physical stock gets overwritten to the pool sum, then that sum is counted as the partner's contribution on the following sync, inflating the shared quantity and pushing ever-larger phantom inventory to every connected sales channel — overselling and corrupted stock numbers that a seller cannot reconcile.
evidence: cross-org-inventory-sync.service.ts:372 `const sourceTotal = (sourceInventory||[]).reduce(...)`, :383 `targetTotal = (targetInventory||[]).reduce(...)`, :387 `newAvailableQty = sourceTotal + targetTotal;`. Then :471 `await this.setVariantLevelsAtLocations(link.TargetVariantId, partnerLocationIds, newAvailableQty, link.TargetOrgId);` — writes the combined total onto the very rows summed into targetTotal. setVariantLevelsAtLocations -> inventoryFacade.setAbsolute sets each level absolute to target (lines 49-72).
fix: Do not overwrite the target org's own physical levels with the aggregated pool total. Keep each org's real per-org stock as the source of truth and store the aggregate only on the link (AvailableQuantity) / a derived display field, or push the aggregate to platforms without persisting it back into InventoryLevels. If a write-back is needed, write the OTHER org's contribution only (target gets sourceTotal), never source+target. Add a regression test that runs two consecutive partnership syncs and asserts the quantity is stable, not growing.
verifier_note: Confirmed the feedback loop. For ShareType 'sync'/'wholesale' (isPartnership, set from invite.shareType — the branch exists specifically to handle these values), newAvailableQty = sourceTotal + targetTotal where targetTotal sums InventoryLevels WHERE ProductVariantId=TargetVariantId AND OrgId=TargetOrgId (:377-383). availableQtyAppliedAtomically stays false so the value is NOT deleted from the update payload. On the source-edit branch, :471 setVariantLevelsAtLocations(TargetVariantId, partnerLocationIds, newAvailableQty, TargetOrgId) routes through inventoryFacade.setAbsolute -> setLevelAbsolute (verified inventory-facade.service.ts:32-34) which sets each matched partner level ABSOLUTE to the combined total. Those written rows belong to the partner (TargetOrgId) at partnerLocationIds, i.e. a subset of exactly the rows targetTotal re-sums on the next event -> targetTotal next cycle holds the inflated combined number, so each subsequent inventory event grows the shared quantity, and multi-location makes setAbsolute write the sum to every location row. SharedQuantity cap at :397 only mitigates if explicitly set. No test covers the partnership aggregation path. The partner's physical stock is overwritten to the pool sum and then recounted as the partner contribution, pushing phantom inventory to channels (oversell). High severity is justified.
corrected_location: src/organizations/services/cross-org-inventory-sync.service.ts:366-388 (combined sum) and :471 (write-back onto target's own levels)

title: Consignment 'absolute newQuantity' path treats one platform location's count as the entire shared availability
impact: A consignment partner who holds the same consigned variant at two locations (or whose pool spans multiple platform locations) will have the shared AvailableQuantity clobbered to just one location's count on every webhook — undercounting (or overcounting) the consigned stock and mis-syncing it to the host and all other partners. Quantity correctness bug on a normal multi-location consignment.
evidence: cross-org-inventory-sync.service.ts:346 `if (request.newQuantity != null ...) { newAvailableQty = request.newQuantity; }`. square.adapter.ts:2234 emits per-location `newQuantity` with `locationId: platformLocationId`. The delta-0 branch right below (line 349-353) DOES recompute from the pool via getPoolQuantityForVariant — showing the intended pool-aware total — but the absolute-newQuantity branch bypasses that and trusts the single-location number.
fix: When a SourcePoolId exists, prefer pool-scoped recomputation (getPoolQuantityForVariant) over the raw single-location newQuantity, or apply the delta (newQuantity - previousLocationQuantity) to the pool rather than overwriting the link with one location's absolute. Only use the absolute newQuantity directly for genuinely single-location links.
verifier_note: Confirmed. The Square adapter emits inventory.sync with newQuantity computed at a single platformLocationId (square.adapter.ts:2223 log 'at Loc ...' and :2234 newQuantity alongside locationId:platformLocationId, one event per inventory-count object), and the listener forwards event.newQuantity unchanged into syncInventoryChange (sync-event-listeners.service.ts:283). In the consignment branch, when request.newQuantity != null it sets newAvailableQty = request.newQuantity verbatim (:346-348), bypassing the pool-aware getPoolQuantityForVariant used in the delta-0 branch immediately below (:349-353) — proving the intended pool-aware total is available but not used here. For a consignment variant stocked at more than one location, the shared AvailableQuantity is clobbered to one location's count on each webhook. The code comment at :344-345 documents the (mistaken) intent of treating the webhook newQuantity as absolute source-of-truth. Harm is real but gated on multi-location consignment (single-location, the common case, is correct), so medium is the right level.
corrected_location: src/organizations/services/cross-org-inventory-sync.service.ts:346-348 (absolute newQuantity verbatim); square.adapter.ts:2227-2237 (per-location emit); sync-event-listeners.service.ts:283 (forward unchanged)

title: syncProductDataChange swallows per-link/per-partner failures, reporting overall success on silent partial data loss
impact: If a host edits a shared product's price/title/images and the update to one partner fork (or the publish to one partner's channel) throws, that partner is left on stale data while the system reports the cross-org sync as completed — silent partial data loss across the org boundary with no surfaced failure for retry or alerting.
evidence: cross-org-inventory-sync.service.ts:1186 `} catch (e) { this.logger.error(\`...Failed to sync data for link ${link.Id}: ${e.message}\`); }` (continues, no aggregation), :1207 same for publish, :1212 `this.logger.log(\`...✅ Sync complete for product ${productId}: updated ${links.length} variants...\`)` reports full count regardless of failures. sync-event-listeners.service.ts:108 logs success unconditionally.
fix: Accumulate per-link failures and either surface them in the method's return (e.g. { updated, failedLinkIds }) and in the operation/activity record, or rethrow an aggregate when any partner update fails so the listener marks the operation degraded and the failure is retryable/visible.
verifier_note: Confirmed as described. syncProductDataChange wraps each partner variant/Products update in try/catch that only logs and continues (:1186-1189) and each per-org publish in another (:1207-1209), returns void, and logs '✅ Sync complete ... updated ${links.length} variants, published ${publishedProducts.size} products' (:1212) regardless of how many sub-steps threw. The caller (sync-event-listeners.service.ts:107-111) try/catches and logs 'Cross-org product sync completed', but since nothing rethrows, a failed partner update or publish is invisible — no surfaced failure for retry/alerting, partner left on stale price/title/images. This is a genuine silent partial-failure / observability gap across the org boundary. It does not directly corrupt quantities or money and the swallow-and-continue is plausibly intentional for partial progress, so the finder's low severity is correct.
corrected_location: src/organizations/services/cross-org-inventory-sync.service.ts:1186-1189 (variant update swallow), :1207-1209 (publish swallow), :1212 (unconditional success log); sync-event-listeners.service.ts:107-111 (caller logs completed)

title: Square order.created handler double-decrements canonical inventory
impact: Every Square sale oversubtracts inventory at double the true rate.
evidence: square.adapter.ts ~2907 updateInventoryLevel(newQuantity) then ~2915 applyDelta({delta:-quantity}); both write InventoryLevels.Quantity.
fix: Keep only one write path.
verifier_note: Verified by reading the cited code and the underlying implementations. In handleSquareOrderCreated, line 2903 reads currentQuantity from the variant/connection/location InventoryLevels row; line 2904 computes newQuantity = max(0, currentQuantity - quantity) (the already-post-sale absolute); line 2907 calls inventoryService.updateInventoryLevel(...newQuantity...) which routes through saveBulkInventoryLevels and writes the absolute Quantity=newQuantity to that exact row. Then lines 2915-2924 call inventoryLedger.applyDelta({delta: -quantity}) targeting the SAME variant/connection/location. applyDelta calls rpc_apply_inventory_delta, whose SQL (migrations/20260524_inventory_ledger_source_of_truth.sql lines 142-198) reads the row's current Quantity into v_current and sets v_new = v_current + p_delta, then UPDATEs InventoryLevels.Quantity = v_new. So the row goes 10 -> 8 (absolute set already deducted the sale) -> 6 (delta deducts it a second time). The sale quantity is subtracted twice on the same projection row. No guard intervenes: the order.created handler passes no lastPlatformUpdateAt so the freshness/stale-skip guard does not block the absolute write; idempotency on applyDelta's sourceId only prevents replaying the SAME applyDelta call, not the unrelated updateInventoryLevel write; the two writes are sequential in one loop iteration. The path is reachable directly from the Square order.created webhook dispatch (line 2498). The correct fix would be to call only ONE of the two (either updateInventoryLevel OR applyDelta), since the ledger's applyDelta already updates the projection. Every Square sale oversubtracts canonical inventory at double the true rate, which can drive stock to zero/negative and cause oversells and cross-platform inventory corruption. Critical is the right severity. Note the same anti-pattern recurs at lines ~3003/3011 and ~3089/3097 (order.updated/refunded paths), but those were out of the finding's cited scope.

title: Swapped arguments to getMappingsByPlatformProductId disable canonical-product reuse on CREATE (variant families split into N products)
impact: When several confirmed CREATE items belong to the same platform product (e.g. a Shopify product with multiple variants each committed as CREATE_NEW, the variant_family path), each variant creates its OWN brand-new canonical Product instead of attaching as variants under one shared canonical product. The seller's catalog is fragmented: one real product becomes many single-variant products, breaking variant grouping, pricing rollups, and later cross-platform propagation.
evidence: Signature (platform-product-mappings.service.ts:588): `async getMappingsByPlatformProductId(platformConnectionId: string, platformProductId: string)`. Correct call (line 1023): `getMappingsByPlatformProductId(connection.Id, match.platformProductId)`. Buggy call (line 1162): `await this.platformProductMappingsService.getMappingsByPlatformProductId(match.platformProductId, connection.Id)` feeding `existingMappingsForPlatformProduct` which gates the product-reuse branch at 1165.
fix: Swap the arguments at line 1162 to (connection.Id, match.platformProductId), matching the signature and the sibling call at 1023. Add a regression test asserting two CREATE items with the same platformProductId resolve to one canonical Product.
verifier_note: Verified directly. Signature is getMappingsByPlatformProductId(platformConnectionId, platformProductId) (platform-product-mappings.service.ts:588, query at 595-596 filters .eq('PlatformConnectionId', arg1).eq('PlatformProductId', arg2)). The dedup call at line 1023 passes (connection.Id, match.platformProductId) correctly. The product-REUSE call at line 1162 passes (match.platformProductId, connection.Id) — arguments swapped — so the query becomes WHERE PlatformConnectionId=<a platform product id> AND PlatformProductId=<a connection uuid>, which can never match. Therefore existingMappingsForPlatformProduct.length is always 0 and the 'Using existing SSSync Product' reuse branch (1165-1183) is dead; every CREATE falls through to saveProduct (1185-1187) creating a brand-new canonical Product. The reuse path IS reachable: line 438-445 shows the processor handles matches whose platformProductId is a shared Shopify product GID across multiple variant matches, which is exactly when reuse should fire. NUANCE on the finder's framing: in the anorha CSV commit path (anorha-import.service.ts:107,129) each variant's ConfirmedMatch carries the VARIANT id as platformProductId, so siblings wouldn't share a key even with correct args — there the split is structural, not caused by this bug. But for the scan-snapshot path where siblings share the product GID, this swapped-arg bug genuinely defeats canonical-product reuse and fragments one product into N single-variant canonical products. Real correctness/data-integrity harm on a reachable path; high stands.

title: Many-to-one CONSOLIDATE silently drops all-but-one platform link (dedup + DB unique constraint on (PlatformConnectionId, ProductVariantId))
impact: If a seller combines, say, 3 duplicate eBay listings into one canonical product, only one listing is actually linked; the other two confirmed 'link' decisions are silently dropped (no error, no activity log of the drop). Those listings remain unmapped/unsynced — inventory and price changes on them never propagate, and on the next scan they re-surface as unresolved, making the consolidation appear to 'not stick'.
evidence: Dedup (mapping.service.ts:671-683): `const key = \`${m.PlatformConnectionId}|${m.ProductVariantId}\`; ... dedupMap.set(key, preferNew ? m : existing);` then `mappingsToUpsert = Array.from(dedupMap.values())`. Constraint confirmed in platform-product-mappings.service.ts:720-721 comment 'the unique constraint in the database is typically on (PlatformConnectionId, ProductVariantId)' and onConflict at line 62.
fix: Consolidation needs a model where many platform products map to one canonical variant: either relax/extend the unique key to include PlatformProductId for many-to-one links, or persist the secondary listings as explicit 'consolidated child' references. At minimum, stop silently dropping: surface the collapsed links in an activity log / warning so the user knows only one listing was linked.
verifier_note: Verified directly. resolveSuggestedCanonicalCollisions (768-789) explicitly keeps every member of a many->1 group pointed at the same canonical so the user can Combine N listings onto one canonical (documented 'Combine many->1', the only intended consolidation). On commit, saveConfirmedMappings (652-664) builds one link row per confirmed 'link' match with ProductVariantId = match.sssyncVariantId. For a genuine consolidation, all N rows share (connection.Id, same sssyncVariantId). The dedup at 671-683 keys on `${PlatformConnectionId}|${ProductVariantId}` and collapses them to exactly one row (PlatformVariantId tiebreak only picks which single survivor). Even bypassing the dedup, the upsert onConflict 'PlatformConnectionId, ProductVariantId' (690) plus the DB unique constraint on (PlatformConnectionId, ProductVariantId) (confirmed by upsertMapping onConflict at line 62 and the comment at 720-721) makes the table physically incapable of holding more than one platform mapping per (connection, canonical variant). So N-1 confirmed link decisions are silently dropped with no error and no activity-log entry; those listings stay unsynced and re-surface unresolved on the next scan. The suggestion-side test (mapping.service.spec.ts:28) covers only that the group is PRESENTED for review, not saveConfirmedMappings, so it does not refute this. Requires the specific scenario of multiple duplicate listings on one connection consolidated onto one variant, but that is precisely the advertised feature failing silently — high is justified.

title: CREATE→LINK SKU dedup is user-scoped, not org-scoped — cross-org tenant leak for multi-org users
impact: For a user who belongs to multiple orgs, importing a product into Org B whose SKU already exists as a canonical variant in Org A causes the CREATE to be silently converted to LINK against Org A's variant (lines 1042-1044), wiring an Org-B platform connection to an Org-A-owned canonical product. That cross-wires inventory/price sync across organizations — a tenant-isolation and data-integrity breach. Even within one org it's wrong if the same SKU legitimately denotes different products.
evidence: initial-sync.processor.ts:1037-1044: `if (match.platformProductSku) { const existingBySku = await this.canonicalProductsService.findVariantBySku(userId, match.platformProductSku); if (existingBySku) { ... match.action = 'link'; match.sssyncVariantId = existingBySku.Id; return this.handleLinkAction(...) } }`. products.service.ts:108-110 shows the query is keyed on UserId only, with no OrgId filter.
fix: Add an org-scoped variant lookup (findVariantBySkuForOrg(orgId, sku)) and use connection.OrgId in handleCreateAction's dedup, falling back to user scope only for legacy null-org connections.
verifier_note: Verified directly. findVariantBySku (products.service.ts:100) queries ProductVariants .eq('UserId', userId).eq('Sku', sku).maybeSingle() (108-110) with NO OrgId filter, while the rest of the import pipeline is org-scoped (findVariantsByOrgId at 76-86 joins Products!inner(OrgId) and filters on resolved OrgId). handleCreateAction (initial-sync.processor.ts:1037-1044) calls findVariantBySku(userId, match.platformProductSku) and, on any hit, mutates match.action='link' + match.sssyncVariantId=existingBySku.Id and routes to handleLinkAction. For a user belonging to multiple orgs, importing a SKU into Org B that already exists as a canonical variant in Org A silently converts the CREATE into a LINK wiring an Org-B connection's platform listing to an Org-A-owned canonical variant — a genuine cross-tenant data/inventory/price cross-wire and isolation breach. maybeSingle would also error/return null on duplicate SKUs across orgs, but the single-hit cross-org case is fully reachable. No OrgId guard exists upstream of this lookup. High stands.

title: SKU-less items get random/unstable IDs — breaks re-import idempotency and duplicates canonical products every re-scan
impact: Every re-scan of an eBay (and any SKU-less Facebook/Square) catalog assigns fresh IDs, so previously-imported listings are treated as brand-new: alreadyMapped never fires, prior IGNORE resolutions don't apply, and confirmed CREATE re-creates duplicate canonical products on each import. Catalog bloats with duplicates and the 'we already handled these' panel is empty on re-import.
evidence: initial-scan.processor.ts:516-517: `id: sku || \`ebay-${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2)}\``. Idempotency dependence: mapping.service.ts:267 `const existingLink = mappingByPlatformProductId.get(String(item.id));` and :274-275 sets alreadyMapped only on id match. Create dedup gated on SKU: initial-sync.processor.ts:1037 `if (match.platformProductSku) {`.
fix: Derive a deterministic stable id for SKU-less listings (e.g. hash of title+price+image, or the platform's native listing id from the Inventory/Trading API) instead of Math.random(). Ensure eBay items always carry their platform listing id as PlatformProductId.
verifier_note: Verified directly. eBay suggestion building uses id: sku || `ebay-${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2)}` (line 517); Facebook/Square/Clover similarly derive id from platform ids but eBay is the random-fallback case for SKU-less items. This id flows to suggestionId = buildSuggestionId('platform_to_anorha', item.id) (mapping.service.ts:351) and to committed match.platformProductId, then is stored as PlatformProductMappings.PlatformProductId (saveConfirmedMappings link rows line 657; handleLinkAction line 844). The idempotency layer keys strictly on PlatformProductId: existingLink = mappingByPlatformProductId.get(String(item.id)) (mapping.service.ts:267) and alreadyMapped is set only when match.Id === existingLink.ProductVariantId for the SAME stored id (274-275); persistImportResolutions/getPriorResolutions also key on PlatformProductId (898). A fresh random id on each re-scan never matches the previously-stored id, so alreadyMapped never fires, prior IGNORE resolutions never apply, and the only remaining CREATE-time dedup is the SKU check guarded by `if (match.platformProductSku)` (initial-sync.processor.ts:1037), which is skipped entirely when SKU is absent. Net: every re-scan of a SKU-less eBay catalog re-creates duplicate canonical products and shows an empty 'already handled' panel. Reachable, harms data/catalog integrity. High stands.

title: Unguarded p.variants.edges.map can crash the entire Shopify scan on one malformed product
impact: A single product with an unexpected/empty variants structure aborts the entire import scan for that connection, so the seller gets no suggestions at all and the connection is marked errored — one bad row poisons the whole catalog import.
evidence: initial-scan.processor.ts:444-446: `return p.variants.edges.map((vEdge: any) => { const variantNode = vEdge.node; ...`. Contrast with the guarded read at line 697 `acc + (product.variants?.edges?.length || 0)` and the warn at initial-sync.processor.ts:483 acknowledging `variants.edges is undefined` happens.
fix: Use `(p.variants?.edges || []).map(...)` and skip/flat-filter products with no variants, mirroring the defensive reads already present at line 697.
verifier_note: Verified directly. variantsForSuggestions for Shopify does p.variants.edges.map(...) with NO optional chaining (line 445), even though the immediately adjacent media read uses p.media?.edges (line 444). If any product in the fetched page has variants or variants.edges undefined, this throws a TypeError. This runs inside the top-level try of process(); the catch at 628-633 sets the connection to 'error' (630) and re-throws (633), so one malformed product aborts the entire scan for that connection and yields zero suggestions. The codebase elsewhere defensively guards this exact shape — analyzePlatformData uses product.variants?.edges?.length || 0 (line 697) — and the create handler warns about 'variants.edges is undefined' (initial-sync.processor.ts:918), confirming the malformed shape is anticipated to occur. Genuine availability/robustness defect (one bad row poisons the whole import), but it is a defensive-coding gap dependent on a malformed Shopify payload rather than a data-corruption/money/tenant issue, so medium (not high) is the correct level.

title: Family-count consolidation gate can split a real variant family when only 2 members come in (inferred-parent threshold of 2)
impact: Some legitimate two-variant families imported via CSV/normalize path are not grouped, producing two standalone canonical products rather than one variant family. Lower severity because the richer scan path supplies explicit platform parentId (initial-scan.processor.ts parentId fields) and bypasses this heuristic; this mainly affects the CSV/normalize entry point.
evidence: anorha-import.service.ts:230-235 family-count<2 strip; inferParentId stem logic at 253-262 `.replace(/[-_:][^-_:]+$/, '')`. The CSV path is the only consumer of buildPlatformItems (normalizeImportItems, line 46).
fix: For the CSV path, prefer explicit parent columns (already handled) and only apply the <2 strip to INFERRED parents, not explicit ones; or lower the singleton-strip aggressiveness when a variant signal (size/color column) is present.
verifier_note: Verified directly. buildPlatformItems infers parentId via inferParentId (238-263) by stripping the last SKU segment (.replace(/[-_:][^-_:]+$/, '') at 256), counts members per inferred parent (224-228), then strips parentId from any family with fewer than 2 members (231-235), demoting it to a simple product. Two genuinely related variants whose SKUs stem to different prefixes land in singleton buckets and are imported as separate simple products instead of one variant family. Confirmed buildPlatformItems is consumed only by normalizeImportItems (line 46), i.e. the CSV/normalize entry point; the richer scan path (initial-scan.processor.ts) sets explicit platform parentId (e.g. parentId: p.id at 454, item.id for Square at 478) and never calls this heuristic, so it is unaffected — matching the finder's own severity reasoning. The threshold-of-2 and stem heuristic are a deliberate conservative trade-off (avoids false family grouping); the failure mode is under-grouping two legitimately related CSV rows, a minor UX/grouping nuisance with no data-loss or money/tenant impact. Low is correct.

title: eBay price/availability/order webhooks resolve mappings with no connection/org scope → cross-tenant data corruption
impact: Money/quantity errors and tenant-isolation leak: one merchant's eBay price/stock/sale event silently overwrites another merchant's price or inventory, or oversells their stock via a phantom ledger decrement. No filter ties the event to the originating account.
evidence: Line 159-163: `.from('PlatformProductMappings').select('ProductVariantId, PlatformConnectionId, ProductId').or(`PlatformInventoryItemId.eq.${sku},PlatformProductId.eq.${itemId}`).single();` — no connection/org predicate. Same pattern lines 212-216 and 274-278. The payload's eBay account id is never used to scope the lookup.
fix: Resolve the eBay PlatformConnection first from the notification's account identifier (the same accountId used in processAccountDeletion), then add `.eq('PlatformConnectionId', connection.Id)` to every mapping lookup. Replace `.single()` with `.maybeSingle()` and handle 0/many. Sanitize SKU/itemId before interpolating into the PostgREST `.or()` filter string.
verifier_note: Verified directly. processItemPriceRevision selects PlatformProductMappings via `.or('PlatformInventoryItemId.eq.${sku},PlatformProductId.eq.${itemId}').single()` (159-163) with NO PlatformConnectionId/OrgId/UserId predicate; same for availability (212-216) and order uses `.eq('PlatformInventoryItemId', sku).single()` (274-278). The handlers never use the originating eBay account id to scope the lookup, and the verified signature (processNotification) only proves the request came from eBay, not which tenant. So a real eBay webhook for seller A whose SKU collides with seller B's PlatformInventoryItemId would update the wrong tenant's ProductVariants.Price / InventoryLevels / ledger, and `.single()` throws when two tenants share the SKU. The control-flow claim and the data/tenant-isolation harm are real and reachable on the verified path. Downgraded from critical to high because of a strong real-world mitigating fact found while verifying finding 6: the eBay signature verification is currently broken (verifySignature hashes a re-stringified body with the wrong scheme), so in production every price/availability/order notification is rejected at lines 73-76 before reaching these handlers — the cross-tenant corruption is presently masked and will only manifest once verification is fixed. It is a genuine latent tenant-isolation/money bug, not a refutation, hence confirmed-high rather than critical.

title: Facebook webhooks have no signature verification but mutate inventory and prices (spoofable)
impact: Security: anyone who learns the public POST /webhook/facebook endpoint can forge a payload (with a known pageId) and drive arbitrary inventory and price mutations on a victim's products — overselling, zeroing stock, or mis-pricing. Unverified webhook directly mutates canonical inventory.
evidence: webhook.controller.ts:636 comment 'For now, accept payload and optionally verify later via APP_SECRET'; the function only does JSON.parse and returns. No crypto/HMAC call anywhere in the facebook branch, unlike verifyShopifyWebhook (line 647) and verifySquareWebhookHmac (line 667). facebook.adapter.ts:798 applyDelta and :895 ProductVariants update confirm the mutation path.
fix: Verify X-Hub-Signature-256 = 'sha256='+HMAC(rawBody, FB_APP_SECRET) with timingSafeEqual in validateFacebookWebhook, throwing UnauthorizedException on mismatch, before the payload is allowed to reach processWebhook.
verifier_note: Verified end-to-end. validateFacebookWebhook (630-645) only does JSON.parse and returns the payload with the explicit comment 'For now, accept payload and optionally verify later via APP_SECRET' — no X-Hub-Signature-256 HMAC check, in contrast to verifyShopifyWebhook (647) and verifySquareWebhookHmac (667) which both compute and timing-safe-compare an HMAC over rawBody. The GET verify route only gates Facebook's subscription handshake, not POST bodies. The accepted payload flows through processWebhookAsync -> syncCoordinator.handleWebhook, which for facebook resolves the connection solely by payload.entry[0].id (the public Page/Catalog id) (sync-coordinator.service.ts:348-366) and then calls adapter.processWebhook. So an attacker who knows a victim's public pageId can POST a forged unsigned payload to the public POST /webhook/facebook and drive arbitrary inventory/price mutations. Spoofable, reachable, mutates canonical data — critical stands.

title: Webhooks whose connection can't be resolved are processed once with no event log → never retried, never reach DLQ (silent loss)
impact: Silent data loss: any webhook that fails while its connection is unresolved (including transient resolution failures) is processed at most once and then forgotten — no retry, no DLQ, no operator visibility. Real inventory/price/order events can be permanently lost during connection-linking windows or DB blips.
evidence: webhook.controller.ts:326 `if (resolvedConnectionId) { webhookEvent = await ...create(...) }`; line 380 `if (webhookEvent) { ...retryQueue.add(...) } else { ...Cannot queue webhook for retry... }`; webhook-retry.processor.ts only ever ingests `{ webhookEventId }` and addToDLQ is called solely from sendMaxRetriesAlert in that processor.
fix: Always create a WebhookEventLog row (allow null PlatformConnectionId, or store the raw shopIdentifier) so failed-but-unresolved webhooks still enter the retry/DLQ pipeline; alternatively push unresolved-connection failures straight to the DLQ with the shopIdentifier for manual linking.
verifier_note: Verified. A WebhookEventLog row is only created when resolvedConnectionId is truthy (326-334). On failure, the catch block queues a retry only when `webhookEvent` exists, else logs 'Cannot queue webhook for retry - no event log entry exists' (380-385). The retry processor's process() is keyed entirely on { webhookEventId } (line 31) and the DLQ is only reached from sendMaxRetriesAlert inside the retry flow (157-193). So a webhook that has no event-log row is invisible to both retry and DLQ — silent loss, as claimed. Downgraded from high to medium on reachability: when a connection truly cannot be resolved, handleWebhook itself returns early (logs and returns, e.g. sync-coordinator.service.ts:309-366,370-373) for every platform rather than throwing, so the common 'unresolved connection' case does NOT enter the catch block and thus loses nothing new (the event was undeliverable regardless). The real silent-loss window is narrower than stated: a transient resolution miss (findConnectionByIdentifier returns null while the connection actually exists) or a throw partway through processing after resolution failed. Genuine but bounded, hence medium.

title: eBay account-deletion POST route skips signature verification and hard-deletes connections/products/mappings
impact: An attacker who knows a victim's eBay username/userId can POST an unsigned deletion payload to the public endpoint and wipe that user's eBay connection plus all associated product mappings and platform-product rows — irreversible data loss and forced disconnect. Tenant data destruction with no authentication.
evidence: ebay.webhooks.controller.ts:77-80 handleAccountDeletionNotification → processAccountDeletion(req.body) with no verifySignature call; the matching+delete loop is ebay-webhook-handler.service.ts:136-142 (`client.from('PlatformConnections').delete().eq('Id', conn.Id)` etc.).
fix: Route the deletion POST through the same signature check as processNotification (require X-EBAY-SIGNATURE and verifySignature/verification-token), or have processAccountDeletion verify before deleting. Note: verifySignature currently hashes JSON.stringify(req.body) rather than the raw body — fix that too so verification actually matches eBay's signature over the raw bytes.
verifier_note: Verified. POST /api/ebay/deletion-webhook (75-80) calls this.webhookHandler.processAccountDeletion(req.body) directly, with no signature/verification token check. The only place the eBay signature is checked is processNotification (59-76), which the deletion route bypasses entirely (the general /notifications and root POST routes do go through it). processAccountDeletion (102-146) fetches all eBay PlatformConnections, filters by PlatformSpecificData.accountId === payload userId/username (126-129), and for each match hard-deletes PlatformProducts, PlatformProductMappings, and the PlatformConnection (139-141). An unauthenticated attacker who knows a victim's eBay username/userId (commonly visible on listings) can POST an unsigned deletion payload and wipe that user's eBay connection plus all mappings/platform-products — irreversible mapping loss and forced disconnect. Confirmed. Kept at high rather than critical: it destroys only the eBay-channel connection and its mappings (re-connectable), not the canonical catalog, and requires knowing the account identifier.

title: eBay verifySignature hashes JSON.stringify(req.body), not the raw payload → valid signatures rejected / verification meaningless
impact: Either all genuine eBay price/availability/order notifications are silently rejected (sync stops working — silent data loss of inbound events), or, combined with the bypass above, the only place that does verify is ineffective. Inbound eBay sync is effectively broken or unverifiable.
evidence: Line 47 `const payload = JSON.stringify(req.body);` then line 48-52 HMAC over that string. eBay (like Shopify/Square) signs the raw body; the controller does not capture rawBody for eBay (req.body is the parsed object).
fix: Capture and verify against the raw request buffer (RawBodyRequest, as the generic webhook controller does for Shopify/Square) rather than re-serializing req.body. Wire rawBody through to verifySignature.
verifier_note: Verified. verifySignature computes an HMAC-SHA256 over `JSON.stringify(req.body)` (47-52) — the parsed-then-reserialized body — keyed on the verification token. eBay actually signs the exact transmitted raw bytes (and its Notification API uses an ECDSA public-key signature, not an HMAC of the verification token), and main.ts captures req.rawBody but verifySignature ignores it. Re-stringifying changes key order/whitespace/number formatting, so the recomputed digest cannot match any non-trivial genuine payload; processNotification then rejects at 73-76 returning status 'signature_invalid' (with HTTP 200), silently dropping every real ITEM_PRICE_REVISION/ITEM_AVAILABILITY/ORDER_CONFIRMATION notification. The finder's effect (genuine inbound eBay sync events silently rejected / verification meaningless) is correct. Medium is right: it breaks inbound eBay sync and drops events silently, but does not itself corrupt money/data; it actually masks finding #1.

title: Realtime-sync endpoints trust connectionId with no ownership check — cross-tenant webhook/sync control
impact: Cross-tenant authorization bypass: read of other tenants' connection metadata and full control over their realtime sync (register/deregister platform webhooks, fire manual sync events), which can corrupt or halt a victim's inventory/price propagation.
evidence: realtime-sync.controller.ts:53-60 getConnectionSyncStatus passes only connectionId; :65-79 enableRealtimeSync passes only connectionId+dto; :84-104 disable; :131-149 trigger. realtime-sync.service.ts:264 `const connection = await this.connectionsService.getConnectionById(connectionId);` (no userId) and :46, :62, :316, :404 same pattern. platform-connections.service.ts:330 `if (userId) { query = query.eq('UserId', userId); }` — scoping only when userId present. Contrast platform-connections.controller.ts:40,56,60 which always pass userId.
fix: Thread the authenticated user.id (and/or org) into every realtime-sync service call and fetch the connection with getConnectionById(connectionId, user.id); throw NotFound/Forbidden when it resolves null. Better: make getConnectionById require userId (no optional) and have callers that legitimately need cross-user access use an explicit separate method.
verifier_note: Verified in source. RealtimeSyncController is registered (sync-engine.module.ts:128) and every :connectionId route (status, enable, disable, test, trigger) captures user only for logging and passes ONLY connectionId to the service. realtime-sync.service.ts calls connectionsService.getConnectionById(connectionId) with a single arg at lines 46, 62, 188, 264, 321, 405. getConnectionById(connectionId, userId?) (platform-connections.service.ts:318-341) only adds .eq('UserId', userId) when userId is truthy (lines 330-332), uses getSupabaseClient (service-role, bypasses RLS), so with one arg it returns ANY user's connection. No ownership guard exists anywhere on this path. The controller is additionally guarded only by the placeholder AuthGuard (common/guards/auth.guard.ts), whose verifyToken is a stub returning a fixed 'placeholder-user-id' for any non-empty token — so auth itself is a no-op here, making the cross-tenant control even more reachable, not less. Any caller can read connection metadata (platform/PlatformSpecificData incl. webhook subscription ids) and enable/disable webhooks or fire manual sync events on any connection UUID. Critical stands.

title: Pools controller operates on poolId/orgId with no membership check — cross-tenant pool read/write/delete
impact: Full cross-tenant CRUD over inventory pools and their location membership, plus the ability to trigger sync jobs against another org's connections — silent data corruption and inventory mis-syncs across tenants.
evidence: pools.controller.ts:50-65 createPool uses body.orgId (req.user.id only logged); :87-95 getPool, :97-130 updatePool, :230-255 deletePool, :196-212 addLocationsToPool pass only poolId; :133-141 getPoolsByOrg uses @Param orgId; :144-165 getAvailableLocations uses @Query orgId then convertClerkOrgIdToDbId. pools.service.ts:177-194 getPoolsByOrg only .eq('org_id', dbOrgId); :196-221 getPoolById only .eq('id', poolId); convertClerkOrgIdToDbId returns a UUID unchanged with no membership check.
fix: Resolve the caller's org from req.user, require membership via organizationsService.requireMembership(dbOrgId, userId) (the pattern already used in connections.controller.ts:66), and have PoolsService.getPoolById/updatePool/deletePool additionally scope by the caller's org_id (.eq('org_id', dbOrgId)) and reject when the pool's org doesn't match.

PoolsController is guarded by the REAL SupabaseAuthGuard (supabase-auth.guard.ts populates req.user.id with a genuine per-user Supabase id via custom HS256/Supabase/Clerk paths), so a real, distinct authenticated user reaches these routes. But no route checks org membership: getPool (:87), updatePool (:97), deletePool (:230), addLocationsToPool (:196), removeLocationFromPool (:215), restorePool (:258), triggerPoolSync (:272) all pass only poolId to PoolsService. PoolsService uses getServiceClient() (pools.service.ts:14-16, service-role → RLS bypassed); getPoolById (:196-221) filters only .eq('id', poolId); getPoolsByOrg (:177-194) only .eq('org_id', dbOrgId); updatePool/deletePool likewise. convertClerkOrgIdToDbId (:18-36) returns a raw UUID verbatim when isUuid() matches, with zero membership validation. So any authenticated user can read/mutate/merge/delete/sync any org's pools and locations by supplying ids. Full cross-tenant CRUD on inventory pools plus triggering reconcile jobs against another org's connections. Critical confirmed.

title: Clover inventory webhook can write a NaN delta into the inventory ledger
impact: Corrupted inventory ledger writes and cross-platform inventory broadcasts from malformed/unexpected webhook payloads — silent inventory data loss or RPC failure on a money-adjacent quantity path.
evidence: clover.adapter.ts:826 `const newQuantity = payload?.data?.object?.quantity ?? payload?.data?.object?.stockCount;`; :829 `if (cloverItemId && newQuantity !== undefined)`; :840 `const delta = Number(newQuantity) - previousQuantity;`; :842 `if (delta !== 0)` then :844 applyDelta. inventory-ledger.service.ts:46-64 forwards delta.delta to rpc with no Number.isFinite guard.
fix: After computing the parsed quantity, do `const q = Number(newQuantity); if (!Number.isFinite(q)) { log+return; }` before the delta math, and add a defensive `if (!Number.isFinite(delta.delta)) throw/skip` at the top of applyDelta so no NaN can ever reach the ledger RPC.
verifier_note: Verified at the cited lines. newQuantity = payload?.data?.object?.quantity ?? payload?.data?.object?.stockCount (clover.adapter.ts:826). The guard at :829 is `cloverItemId && newQuantity !== undefined` — it only rejects undefined, not non-numeric. At :840 `const delta = Number(newQuantity) - previousQuantity`; a non-numeric/object/empty-but-defined quantity yields Number(...)=NaN → delta=NaN. The branch guard at :842 is `if (delta !== 0)`, and NaN !== 0 is true, so :844 calls inventoryLedger.applyDelta({ delta: NaN, ... }). InventoryLedgerService.applyDelta (:46-64) has no Number.isFinite check and forwards delta.delta as p_delta to rpc_apply_inventory_delta; over JSON-RPC NaN serializes to null, corrupting a real variant's ledger write, and :865/:874 also broadcast newQuantity:NaN. Real, reachable, money-adjacent. Downgraded critical->high: it requires a malformed/unexpected quantity field inside an otherwise valid (signature-gated) Clover webhook rather than a trivially attacker-chosen value, but the value is platform/payload-controlled and there is genuinely no finite guard on a ledger write, so high is appropriate.

title: Sync-coordinator pushes parseFloat(Price) with no NaN guard to platform updateProduct
impact: A null/blank canonical price can propagate as NaN into an outbound platform price update, producing a rejected write or a corrupted price on the live listing — inconsistent with the guarded sibling code paths.
evidence: sync-coordinator.service.ts:1594 `Price: parseFloat(v.Price as any),`; compare :700 `Price: v.Price ? parseFloat(v.Price as any) : 0,` and :2026 same guarded form; the unguarded variant array feeds adapter.updateProduct at :2075.
fix: Mirror the sibling guard: `Price: v.Price != null ? parseFloat(v.Price as any) : 0` and drop the field (or skip the price sync) when the result is not finite, so a missing canonical price never overwrites a live listing price with NaN.
verifier_note: Verified. Line 1594 is `Price: parseFloat(v.Price as any)` with no null/empty fallback, while siblings at :700 and :2026 use `v.Price ? parseFloat(...) : 0`. If v.Price is null/empty the canonical Price becomes NaN. Defect is real and inconsistent with guarded siblings. However the finder mis-cited the downstream sink: the canonicalVariantsForAdapter array built at :1586 feeds adapter.createProduct at :1679 (an outbound product CREATE), not adapter.updateProduct at :2075 — that updateProduct call consumes a different array built at :2016 whose price is the guarded form at :2026. So the NaN-price risk is on the CREATE path, not the update path the finder described. Net harm (a blank canonical price propagating as NaN into an outbound platform write → rejected or corrupted price) is real but lower-likelihood (requires null Price on a variant being pushed) and bounded; medium is correct, location corrected to createProduct@1679.
