title: Order/cancel/refund webhooks look up mappings by numeric variant_id against the Product-GID column, so inventory never adjusts
impact: Selling, cancelling, or refunding an item on Shopify never updates canonical inventory via webhook. Stock drifts: items remain 'in stock' in Anorha and on every other synced platform after a Shopify sale (oversell), and cancellations/refunds never return stock. This is the core promise of an inventory sync engine, broken silently with no error surfaced.
evidence: adapter line 1956-1959: `getMappingsByPlatformProductId(connection.Id, variantId.toString())` where variantId = item.variant_id (numeric). service line 595-596: `.eq('PlatformConnectionId', ...).eq('PlatformProductId', platformProductId)`. Contrast handleInventoryUpdate (line 1872) which correctly builds `gid://shopify/InventoryItem/${inventoryItemId}` and uses getMappingByPlatformVariantInventoryItemId.
fix: Resolve the variant by its variant GID, not the product GID column. Build `gid://shopify/ProductVariant/${variantId}` and look up via a PlatformVariantId-keyed query (or getMappingByPlatformIdentifiers / a getMappingByPlatformVariantId helper). Apply to all three handlers. Add a test asserting a numeric variant_id from an orders/create payload resolves to a mapping.
verifier_note: Verified against actual code. getMappingsByPlatformProductId (service:588) filters with exact .eq('PlatformProductId', platformProductId) and does NO numeric/GID normalization. PlatformProductId is provably stored as the Shopify Product GID (adapter:351, explicit comment 'Shopify Product GID'; PlatformVariantId at 352 holds the Variant GID). All three order/cancel/refund handlers pass a bare numeric line_item.variant_id (.toString()) into this product-GID-keyed lookup. A numeric string like '456' can never equal 'gid://shopify/Product/123', so mappings is always [], the length>0 guard fails, and the decrement/restore is silently skipped. The contrast is real: handleInventoryUpdate (1872) builds a GID and uses getMappingByPlatformVariantInventoryItemId which even tries both numeric+GID forms. A correct variant-based method (getMappingByPlatformVariantIdAndConnection:492, with idsToTry + JSONB fallback) exists and is unused here. Reachable on every Shopify sale/cancel/refund webhook; breaks the core inventory-sync promise with no error surfaced (errors are swallowed per-item). Critical confirmed.

title: updateProduct ignores productSet userErrors and reports success when Shopify rejected variant/price/option changes
impact: Silent data loss / divergence on edits: a price change, new variant, or option change that Shopify rejects is reported as a successful update. The canonical store believes Shopify matches it when it does not, suppressing retries and future conflict detection. The user sees 'synced' but the storefront shows stale data.
evidence: api-client line 2062-2065 returns userErrors. adapter line 747-752 only branches on `!updated`; no reference to updateResult.userErrors anywhere in updateProduct (lines 672-858).
fix: After `const updated = updateResult.product;`, push updateResult.userErrors into results.errors (mirroring createProduct), and treat a non-empty userErrors list as a failed/partial push so the sync engine can retry or flag it. Do not return a clean success when userErrors is non-empty.
verifier_note: Verified. updateProductAsync returns { product, userErrors } (api-client:2062-2065), and productSet can return a (poll-resolved/partial) product alongside non-empty userErrors. updateProduct (adapter:747-751) reads only updateResult.product and branches solely on !updated; grep confirms userErrors is never referenced anywhere in updateProduct (672-858). createProduct DOES check (440-446) and throws on userErrors, so the asymmetry is real. When Shopify rejects some variant/price/option changes but still returns a product, results.errors stays empty and the method returns success-shaped, so the sync engine records 'Success' for a partially/fully-rejected edit. Note: a hard failure that throws is caught at 852 and recorded as an error, so the silent case is specifically the partial-apply / poll path — still a genuine silent data-divergence bug on edits. High confirmed.

title: No Shopify GraphQL throttle/cost handling or retry; bulk multi-variant pushes fail piecemeal under rate limits
impact: On any non-trivial catalog (or a burst of inventory updates), pushes partially fail with THROTTLED. Inventory and price writes are dropped per-variant with no automatic recovery, producing intermittent, hard-to-reproduce stock/price drift. Read pagination loops (fetchAllRelevantData) likewise have no cost-aware pacing and can throttle mid-sync.
evidence: api-client line 2157-2166: requestWithConnection has no cost/throttle/retry logic. adapter updateInventoryLevels line 1140-1156: per-variant call inside try/catch counts thrown throttle as a swallowed failure.
fix: Wrap requestWithConnection with a retry-on-THROTTLED loop honoring extensions.cost.throttleStatus (currentlyAvailable / restoreRate) and the THROTTLED error code with exponential backoff. Batch inventory updates into a single setInventoryQuantities call (the API accepts an array) rather than one request per variant.
verifier_note: Verified. requestWithConnection (2157-2166) only throws on response.errors; no throttle/cost/retry/backoff. A repo-wide grep for throttle/cost/retry/429/backoff/sleep/rate-limit found only an unrelated 'cost?: string' type field (235). The shopifyApi() init (943-950) sets no retry option, and @shopify/shopify-api's GraphQL client does not auto-retry THROTTLED by default. updateInventoryLevels (1140-1156) calls setInventoryQuantities once per variant inside a per-item try/catch; a thrown THROTTLED is caught at 1153, counted failureCount++ and pushed to errors with no backoff/retry, so that variant's write is dropped. Facts all hold. Downgraded high->medium: sequential single-variant calls and one-page-at-a-time read pagination rarely throttle for single-store sync, the failure is counted/logged (not fully invisible), and it only bites under sustained bursts. Real resilience gap causing intermittent drift, but not high under typical load.

title: updateInventoryLevels does not clamp negative quantities; create paths do — asymmetric, and absolute-set has no concurrency guard
impact: When a variant is oversold (negative on-hand), the steady-state inventory push silently fails for that variant instead of pushing 0/out-of-stock to Shopify, leaving the Shopify storefront showing the last positive quantity — i.e. continuing to advertise stock that doesn't exist. The clamping intent stated in the create/mapper comments is not honored on the path that runs most often. Separately, ignoreCompareQuantity:true means this is an unconditional absolute set with no optimistic-concurrency check, so a concurrent Shopify-side change can be clobbered.
evidence: adapter line 1143: `quantity: Math.round(level.Quantity)` (no clamp). Compare mapper line 485/632 and adapter line 1015 which all use Math.max(0, ...). api-client line 2614-2617: name:'available', ignoreCompareQuantity:true.
fix: Clamp in updateInventoryLevels: `quantity: Math.max(0, Math.round(level.Quantity || 0))`, matching the create/mapper boundary policy. Optionally drop ignoreCompareQuantity and supply compareQuantity for optimistic concurrency where the prior level is known.
verifier_note: Verified. updateInventoryLevels sends quantity: Math.round(level.Quantity) at adapter:1143 with no Math.max(0,...). The create/mapper paths all clamp: mapper:485 and mapper:632 use Math.max(0, ...) with explicit comments 'Clamp at the sync boundary', and adapter:1015 uses Math.max(0, level.Quantity || 0) with a comment stating the ledger stores true (possibly negative) on-hand but external platforms expect availableQuantity >= 0. So the clamping intent is documented and the steady-state path violates it. api-client sets name:'available', ignoreCompareQuantity:true (2614-2617) = unconditional absolute set, no optimistic-concurrency guard. When canonical on-hand is negative (oversold), Shopify rejects negative available -> userError -> counted failure (1145-1150) and the write is lost rather than clamped to 0, leaving stale positive stock on the storefront. Medium confirmed: only triggers in the negative/oversold state, but on the most-run path and contrary to the code's own stated invariant.

title: createProduct returns success with an empty variant map when the post-create fetch-back fails, stranding the product with no mappings/inventory/prices
impact: A transient read failure (or the >50-variant cap in SHOP-6) immediately after creation produces a live Shopify product with no inventory, no prices applied, and no mapping rows in Anorha — effectively an orphaned listing that the sync engine considers 'created/success' and won't repair, while inventory webhooks for it can't be matched.
evidence: adapter line 460-465: `return { platformProductId, platformVariantIds };` (empty map) inside the `if (!createdProducts || ... )` branch, with comment 'requires careful handling downstream' but no throw and no caller-visible error flag.
fix: Treat an empty/failed fetch-back as a hard error: throw so the publish flow can retry mapping resolution, or retry fetchProductsByIds with backoff before giving up. At minimum return an error indicator the sync engine inspects rather than a success-shaped object.
verifier_note: Verified. After createProductAsync succeeds (product exists on Shopify), the fetch-back at 459 feeds the !createdProducts || length===0 || !variants?.edges guard at 460; on failure the code logs an error and returns { platformProductId, platformVariantIds } with platformVariantIds still empty, with NO throw and no caller-visible error flag (comment even says 'requires careful handling downstream'). The pre-create mapping loop at 494 iterates Object.entries(platformVariantIds) = zero entries, so no mapping rows are created; the method returns success-shaped. Result: a live Shopify product with no mappings that the sync engine treats as created/success and won't repair, and whose webhooks can't be matched. One nuance reducing scope: the inventory-setup block at 514+ uses an independent getProductById (518, first:100) rather than platformVariantIds, so initial inventory can still partially set on the create path — the strict 'no inventory' part is weaker, but the mapping/price loss is real. Transient read failure required; medium confirmed.

title: Variant fetch-back query caps variants at first:50 with no pagination, dropping mappings/inventory for products with 51–100 variants
impact: Large-variant products (apparel size/color matrices commonly approach 100) silently lose sync for the tail variants: those variants have no mapping, so their inventory/price never push and their inbound webhooks can't be matched. Data loss scales with variant count and is invisible (no error).
evidence: api-client GET_PRODUCTS_BY_IDS_QUERY line 552-573 uses `variants(first: 50)` with no cursor/loop; consumers fetchProductsByIds (line 1902) which uses that query. createProduct maps off this result (adapter line 469-485). Note createProduct's separate getProductById (line 518) uses first:100, but the SKU->GID map and pre-created mappings come from the first:50 fetchProductsByIds result.
fix: Paginate variants in fetchProductsByIds (loop on variants.pageInfo until hasNextPage is false, as fetchAllRelevantData already does for the main sync) or raise to first:100 with explicit handling, and assert mapped variant count equals canonical variant count before declaring success.
verifier_note: Verified. GET_PRODUCTS_BY_IDS_QUERY uses variants(first: 50) (line 573) and DOES select pageInfo{hasNextPage endCursor}, but fetchProductsByIds (1902-1960) issues a single client.request and never inspects pageInfo or loops — confirmed no pagination. createProduct builds its canonical-variant->GID map and pre-creates PlatformProductMappings from this result (adapter:469 onward over createdShopifyProductNode.variants.edges), and updateProduct also uses fetchProductsByIds (717). Shopify allows up to 100 variants/product, so variants 51-100 are absent: no GID map entry, no pre-created mapping, no price push, and their inbound webhooks can't be matched. As the finder noted, createProduct's separate getProductById (518) uses first:100 so initial inventory for tail variants may still set on the create path, but the SKU->GID map and mappings come from the first:50 fetch and miss them. Silent, scales with variant count. Medium confirmed — only affects >50-variant products (uncommon but real for apparel size/color matrices).

title: Order webhooks resolve inventory by ITEM id but Square line items carry the VARIATION id → inventory never decrements on a sale
impact: Square POS / online sales do not reduce canonical inventory through the order webhook path. Items keep showing as in-stock across all synced channels after they sell on Square, causing oversells. The failure is silent (warn log only).
evidence: square.adapter.ts:2878 `const catalogObjectId = item.catalog_object_id;` then :2888 `getMappingsByPlatformProductId(connection.Id, catalogObjectId)`. Contrast :2191 `const platformVariantId = inventoryData.catalog_object_id; // This is Square ItemVariation ID` then :2199 `getMappingByPlatformVariantIdAndConnection(platformVariantId, connection.Id)`. Lookup column proof: platform-product-mappings.service.ts:595-596 `.eq('PlatformConnectionId', ...).eq('PlatformProductId', platformProductId)`.
fix: In the three order handlers, resolve the mapping by variation id: use `getMappingByPlatformVariantIdAndConnection(catalogObjectId, connection.Id)` (as the inventory webhook does), optionally falling back to the product-id lookup for items without variation-level catalog ids.
verifier_note: Verified directly. getMappingsByPlatformProductId filters strictly on PlatformProductId (.eq('PlatformProductId', ...) at lines 595-596), which stores the Square ITEM id (syncFromPlatform sets PlatformProductId=platformProductId at 209 and PlatformVariantId=normalized variation id at 210; createProduct pre-mapping at 452-454 likewise). The order handlers pass item.catalog_object_id into this ITEM-id lookup, but a Square order line item's catalog_object_id is the ITEM_VARIATION id — the same file documents exactly this for the inventory webhook ('This is Square ItemVariation ID' at 2191) where it correctly uses getMappingByPlatformVariantIdAndConnection. So for any normally-mapped product the lookup returns zero rows -> 'No mapping found' warn -> no decrement. Handlers are live-wired in the webhook dispatcher (cases at 2497-2504), so the path is reachable. One temper on impact (not on the defect): stock for inventory-tracked items can still be reconciled via the separate inventory.count.updated webhook which uses the correct variant lookup, so 'oversell' is not guaranteed for every merchant; but the order-webhook decrement is genuinely broken. Severity remains high.

title: Order handlers double-apply inventory: absolute set via updateInventoryLevel AND a ledger delta of the same magnitude
impact: For any order whose mapping resolves, canonical inventory is decremented (or restored) by 2x the sold quantity, corrupting stock counts and the immutable ledger. Combined with finding sq-order-mapping-wrong-key this is currently masked (lookup usually fails), but it becomes an active corruption the moment the lookup is fixed or a product happens to match.
evidence: square.adapter.ts:2907-2912 `await this.inventoryService.updateInventoryLevel(mapping.ProductVariantId, connection.Id, squareLocation, newQuantity)` followed by :2915 `await this.inventoryLedger.applyDelta({ ... delta: -quantity ...})`. updateInventoryLevel sets an absolute level (inventory.service.ts:355-384). applyDelta mutates the projection by delta (inventory-ledger.service.ts:46-64).
fix: Use a single source of truth: drop the manual `updateInventoryLevel(newQuantity)` call and rely solely on `applyDelta({ delta: -quantity })` (the ledger already clamps/records and emits). Apply the same single-path fix to the updated and refunded handlers.
verifier_note: Verified. handleSquareOrderCreated computes newQuantity=max(0,current-quantity) and writes it as an ABSOLUTE value via inventoryService.updateInventoryLevel (which routes to saveBulkInventoryLevels -> a freshness-guarded upsert that sets Quantity absolutely, inventory.service.ts:355-393), then immediately calls inventoryLedger.applyDelta({delta:-quantity}) which the service documents as updating the SAME InventoryLevels projection by the delta (inventory-ledger.service.ts:46-64, 'append the immutable ledger row + update the InventoryLevels projection'). Both target the same variant/connection/location row, and the order path passes no lastPlatformUpdateAt so the freshness guard does not suppress the absolute write -> net result current-2*quantity. Same pattern in updated (+2x restore) and refunded (+2x restore). Correctly noted as currently masked by sq-order-mapping-wrong-key (the lookup usually fails before reaching this code), but it is live corruption the moment the lookup is fixed or an id happens to match. High.

title: order.updated with state COMPLETED restores (adds back) inventory as if the order were canceled
impact: Every Square order that transitions to COMPLETED inflates inventory by the sold quantity, directly contradicting the sale and causing oversells across synced channels.
evidence: square.adapter.ts:2976 `if (order.state === 'CANCELED' || order.state === 'COMPLETED')`; :3001 `const newQuantity = currentQuantity + quantity;`; :3011-3016 `applyDelta({ ... delta: quantity, reason: 'order' ...})`.
fix: Only restore on cancel/void states (CANCELED, and handle voided/returned explicitly). Remove COMPLETED from the restore branch; a COMPLETED order should not change inventory beyond the original order.created deduction.
verifier_note: Verified. handleSquareOrderUpdated branches on `if (order.state === 'CANCELED' || order.state === 'COMPLETED')` (2976) and then for each line item computes newQuantity=currentQuantity+quantity (3001) and applyDelta({delta:quantity}) (3011-3020) — i.e. it ADDS the sold quantity back. COMPLETED is the normal terminal state of a fulfilled Square order, not a cancellation, so treating it identically to CANCELED re-stocks just-sold items. Handler is live-wired (case 'order.updated' at 2500). The defect logic is unambiguous. Real-world activation is gated behind sq-order-mapping-wrong-key (lookup must resolve to reach the restore), same masking as the double-apply finding, but the intent/logic is clearly wrong and would inflate inventory once mappings resolve. High.

title: createVariant and deleteVariant ignore Square's 200-with-errors response and report fake success
impact: deleteVariant can silently no-op (variant remains live on Square while Anorha believes it deleted) and createVariant can mask the true Square error. The sync engine treats these as success, leading to drift between Anorha and Square with no error surfaced.
evidence: createVariant square.adapter.ts:1735-1741 calls batchUpsertCatalogObjects; next lines 1744-1748 only read `response.id_mappings`, never `response.errors`. deleteVariant square.adapter.ts:1875-1881 calls batchUpsertCatalogObjects then :1883 logs success with no error inspection.
fix: After both batchUpsert calls, check `if (response.errors?.length) throw new InternalServerErrorException(...)` (mirroring createProduct:383 / updateProduct:1364) before declaring success / extracting id_mappings.
verifier_note: Verified the asymmetry. The API client batchUpsertCatalogObjects returns Square's 200-with-errors body without throwing — it only logs a warning and returns errors in the response (square-api-client.service.ts:1474-1494); it throws only on transport/non-2xx in the catch (1495-1499). createProduct (383-396) and updateProduct (1364-1369) both inspect response.errors; createVariant (1744-1748) reads only response.id_mappings and never response.errors, and deleteVariant (1875-1883) calls batchUpsert then logs 'Successfully deleted' and returns void with no error inspection at all. So deleteVariant can silently no-op (variant remains live on Square while Anorha believes it deleted) — a genuine fake-success. createVariant is partially overstated: on a business error with no id_mappings it still THROWS (the misleading 'Failed to get new variation ID' at 1748), so it is not silent — the real harm there is a masked/misleading error, plus a theoretical partial-success slip-through. Downgraded high->medium: deleteVariant's silent no-op is real but requires a Square-side business failure (e.g. VERSION_MISMATCH) to manifest, and createVariant does surface an error rather than reporting success.
corrected_location: deleteVariant square.adapter.ts:1875-1883 (silent no-op); createVariant square.adapter.ts:1735-1748 (misleading error)

title: Order line-item quantity parsed with parseInt, truncating fractional (weight-priced) Square quantities
impact: For merchants selling by weight/measure, the inventory deduction is wrong (rounded down) or skipped, under-decrementing stock. Lower severity because most Square catalog sales are whole-unit.
evidence: square.adapter.ts:2879 `const quantity = parseInt(item.quantity) || 0;` (repeated at 2982, 3068), combined with the `quantity === 0` early-skip at 2881/2984/3070.
fix: Use `parseFloat`/Number for the quantity and only round at the Square inventory-write boundary (as updateInventoryLevels already does), or carry the fractional delta through the ledger if the schema supports it.
verifier_note: Verified. All three order handlers parse the line-item quantity with parseInt(item.quantity)||0. Square order line item quantity is a decimal string and is fractional for weight/measure-priced catalog items; parseInt('1.5')=1 (under-decrement) and parseInt('0.5')=0, which then hits the `quantity === 0` early-continue (2881/2984/3070) and is dropped entirely. Mechanism is exactly as described. Severity low is appropriate: most Square catalog sales are whole-unit, and this whole sub-system is currently gated behind the broken mapping lookup (sq-order-mapping-wrong-key) so it does not bite in production today, but the parsing bug is real and would surface for weight-priced merchants once the lookup is fixed.

title: Webhook SKU→mapping lookup is not scoped to the connection (cross-tenant corruption + .single() crash on SKU collision)
impact: Cross-tenant inventory/price corruption: seller A's eBay sale or price change silently rewrites seller B's canonical price or stock, then fans out cross-platform via handleCanonicalProductUpdate. On any SKU collision the notification is lost entirely. This is the most material bug because it both corrupts data and breaks for real multi-tenant usage.
evidence: Line 162: `.or(`PlatformInventoryItemId.eq.${sku},PlatformProductId.eq.${itemId}`)` then line 163 `.single()` — no connection/org filter. Same pattern lines 215-216 and 277-278. The notification payload carries no connection id and none is resolved before the query.
fix: Resolve the owning eBay connection from the notification (eBay user/account id in payload, matched against PlatformConnections.PlatformSpecificData.accountId like processAccountDeletion already does), then add `.eq('PlatformConnectionId', conn.Id)` to every mapping lookup and use `.maybeSingle()` (or iterate rows) instead of `.single()`.
verifier_note: Verified directly. The single global webhook endpoint POST /api/ebay/notifications (ebay.webhooks.controller.ts line 116-123) hands the raw eBay payload to processNotification; the eBay payload carries only metadata.topic + notification.data (itemId/sku/price/availability) and NO internal PlatformConnectionId. All three handlers resolve the canonical variant by SKU/ItemID with zero connection/org/user filter: processItemPriceRevision .or(`PlatformInventoryItemId.eq.${sku},PlatformProductId.eq.${itemId}`).single() (161-163), processItemAvailability same (214-216), processOrderConfirmation .eq('PlatformInventoryItemId', sku).single() (276-278). Mappings ARE per-connection (PlatformConnectionId column exists, used in processAccountDeletion line 140) and there is no global unique constraint on PlatformInventoryItemId across connections, so two sellers can hold the same human SKU. Both claimed consequences hold: (a) on a single match for the wrong tenant, ProductVariants.Price / InventoryLevels get overwritten and fan out via handleCanonicalProductUpdate (lines 189-193, 247-251, 326-330); (b) on a multi-row SKU collision, .single() errors and processNotification's try/catch (96-99) swallows it, dropping the notification. eBay seller-scoped SKUs are genuinely collision-prone. This is the most material bug — tenant isolation + money/inventory integrity. The auto-generated ANORHA-<8hex> SKUs derive from distinct variant UUIDs so they themselves rarely collide, but seller-supplied SKUs do, which is enough.

title: AVAILABILITY notification writes InventoryLevels directly, bypassing the event-sourced ledger (projection desync)
impact: eBay-driven stock changes are lost or cause oscillation: the availability webhook sets a quantity that the next order/sync delta overwrites because the ledger never saw it. Inventory shown to the user and pushed to other platforms becomes wrong and non-auditable.
evidence: Lines 225-235: direct `.upsert({ ProductVariantId, PlatformConnectionId, PlatformLocationId: null, Quantity: newQuantity ... })` with NO ledger call — contrast with lines 302-312 (same file) and adapter lines 1398-1407 which deliberately route through the ledger.
fix: Replace the direct upsert with inventoryLedger.setAbsolute({ variantId, connectionId, locationId: <resolved level location>, target: newQuantity, reason: 'sync', actor: 'ebay-webhook', sourceId: `ebay_avail_${itemId}_${sku}` }) so the projection stays consistent with the ledger.
verifier_note: Verified. processItemAvailability does a raw client.from('InventoryLevels').upsert({ ... Quantity: newQuantity, PlatformLocationId: null ... }) (225-235) with NO ledger call. The ledger service explicitly documents InventoryLevels as a projection of the immutable ledger (inventory-ledger.service.ts line 51-52: 'The ledger is the source of truth; InventoryLevels is its projection'). Every other inventory writer routes through the ledger RPC: the order path in this same file uses inventoryLedger.applyDelta (302-312), and syncSingleProductFromPlatform in ebay.adapter.ts uses inventoryLedger.setAbsolute with the explicit comment 'instead of a direct Quantity overwrite, so platform-pull stays event-sourced' (1389-1408). A direct projection upsert here desyncs the projection from the ledger: the next applyDelta computes new_quantity from the ledger's last value under a row lock, silently reverting this webhook's number, and conservation/audit is broken. The PlatformLocationId: null write also won't match the location key other writers resolve via the level's actual PlatformLocationId. Reachable on any ITEM_AVAILABILITY notification. The author clearly knew the right pattern (used it 13 lines down in the order path) and skipped it here.

title: updateInventoryLevels forwards raw (negative/NaN) quantity to eBay; create/update paths clamp but this one doesn't
impact: When a variant is oversold (negative ledger quantity) or has a malformed level, the eBay quantity push 400s and that variant's stock never updates on eBay — the listing stays live at a stale quantity, risking further oversell. Inconsistent with the clamp the create path already applies.
evidence: adapter line 1121 `const quantity = level?.Quantity ?? 0;` → api-client line 597 `quantity,`. Compare adapter line 435 `const qty = Math.max(0, Number(level.Quantity || 0));` and comment at lines 433-434.
fix: In updateInventoryLevels compute `const quantity = Math.max(0, safeParseQuantity(level?.Quantity, 0));` (mirror the create-path clamp + use the existing safeParseQuantity helper to kill NaN).
verifier_note: Verified. updateInventoryLevels reads const quantity = level?.Quantity ?? 0 (1121) and passes it straight to api.updateInventoryQuantity, which PUTs shipToLocationAvailability: { quantity } unchanged (ebay-api-client.service.ts 596-598). ?? 0 only catches null/undefined; a negative survives. Negatives are genuinely reachable: the ledger allows on-hand to go negative on oversell (inventory-ledger.service.ts line 63, p_allow_negative defaults true; interface comment lines 15-18), and level.Quantity is read from the InventoryLevels projection of that ledger. The create/fallback path deliberately clamps with Math.max(0, Number(level.Quantity || 0)) and the comment 'eBay's quantity field expects >= 0' (1433-435), but this push path has no clamp. Sending a negative quantity 400s on eBay's PUT inventory_item and that variant's stock never updates, leaving a stale live quantity and oversell risk. Severity medium is right: real but narrow (only fires on a truly oversold variant); the NaN angle is weak since Quantity is a numeric column, but the negative angle alone confirms it.

title: createProduct is non-atomic across variants: throws mid-loop leaving some variants already published
impact: Partial publish: a multi-variant product can leave orphan live eBay listings while the app believes publish failed, leading to duplicate listings on retry and inconsistent mapping state. Inconsistent with the resilient per-variant reporting updateProduct already uses.
evidence: Lines 913-916 `} catch (error) { this.logger.error(...); throw error; }` inside the per-variant loop; successful variants at lines 887-906 are already published+mapped before a later variant throws.
fix: Mirror updateProduct: collect per-variant results, push {variantId, success:false, error} on failure and continue; return the successfully published platformVariantIds with a partial-failure summary so the caller can surface partial success rather than total failure.
verifier_note: Verified. createProduct loops variants; each iteration calls createOrReplaceInventoryItem + createOffer + publishOffer and pre-creates the mapping (838-906) BEFORE the next iteration. The catch at 913-916 does this.logger.error(...); throw error; — re-throwing on any single variant failure aborts the whole loop and never reaches the return at 920-924. So if variant 3 of 5 throws (e.g. publishOffer's EBAY_SELLER_SETUP_REQUIRED at api-client 545, or a category/aspect error), variants 1-2 are already live on eBay with mappings, yet the caller sees total failure and gets no platformVariantIds. updateProduct, by contrast, pushes per-variant {success:false,error} and continues (1016-1019), proving the codebase has a resilient pattern this path doesn't use. Partial publish leaves orphan live listings and risks duplicates on retry. Medium is appropriate: data inconsistency, not money loss, and only on multi-variant products where a middle variant fails.

title: getAccessToken swallows refresh failure and returns the known-expired token
impact: Expired-token connections surface as opaque 500s on publish/inventory-push instead of a clear 'reconnect eBay' signal, and a transient refresh hiccup yields a guaranteed-failing call rather than a retry. Masks an auth condition the sync engine should act on.
evidence: Lines 113-127: refresh in try; catch logs only; control falls to `return token;` at line 127 with the token already known expired (line 113 condition `now >= expiresAt - bufferMs`).
fix: If refresh fails on a token that is already past expiry, rethrow an UnauthorizedException so the caller/sync engine can flag the connection for re-auth; optionally add a single retry-on-401 in the request wrapper that forces refresh before giving up.
verifier_note: Verified. getAccessToken enters the refresh branch only when now >= expiresAt - bufferMs (113), i.e. the token is already past its (buffered) expiry. If refreshAccessToken throws, the catch (120-124) only logs and falls through to return token (127) — returning the token it just computed as expired. The rationalizing comment ('token might still work') is contradicted by the guard that put us here. The subsequent API call then 401s deep inside createOffer/publishOffer/updateInventoryQuantity, all of which rethrow a generic InternalServerErrorException (api-client 506, 554, 619) with no 401-specific retry-after-refresh and no 'reconnect eBay' signal. So a refresh failure surfaces as an opaque 500 on publish/inventory-push and a transient refresh hiccup yields a guaranteed-failing call. Medium: degrades error clarity and reliability, but doesn't corrupt data or money; the token occasionally still working (eBay's grace window) is the only thing keeping this from being higher.

title: createProduct defaults publish quantity to 1 when stock is 0/unknown — publishes sellable stock the seller doesn't have
impact: Oversell risk: a product with zero real stock goes live on eBay as buyable (qty 1); a buyer can purchase an item the seller doesn't have, triggering an eBay sold notification and cross-platform decrement of phantom inventory.
evidence: Lines 829-833 `if (quantity <= 0) { ... quantity = 1; }`; identical at lines 964-968 and 1439-1443. The fallback at lines 824-827 already tries org totals before this, so reaching here means genuinely zero known stock.
fix: Don't fabricate quantity=1. If quantity is 0, either skip publish (keep the offer unpublished) or publish with quantity 0 / out-of-stock handling so the listing isn't sellable; surface 'no stock to publish' to the caller instead of silently listing 1.
verifier_note: Verified at all three sites. createProduct (829-833), updateProduct (964-968), and createVariant (1439-1443) each force quantity = 1 when the resolved quantity is <= 0, after the org-total fallback at 824-827 (and equivalents) has already been tried — so reaching this branch means genuinely zero known stock. The item is then published live and buyable on eBay (createOrReplaceInventoryItem availability { quantity:1 } + createOffer + publishOffer). The finder's claim that eBay does not actually require quantity > 0 is a side point I can't fully adjudicate from this repo, but the core defect is independent of it: an out-of-stock product is published as a sellable 1-unit listing, creating a real oversell path where a buyer purchases an item the seller doesn't have, which then triggers an eBay sold notification and a cross-platform phantom decrement. Reachable for any brand-new variant with no inventory level yet. Medium is correct — oversell risk, but only on genuinely-zero-stock publishes and bounded to 1 unit per listing.

title: createOffer 'already exists' recovery swallows the update failure and proceeds with possibly-stale price
impact: On a re-publish/idempotent retry, an item can go live at the previously listed price rather than the new canonical price, with the system reporting success — a silent price-integrity gap on the update branch.
evidence: Lines 492-502: `try { await this.updateOffer(...pricingSummary...) } catch (updateErr) { this.logger.warn(...) }` then unconditionally `return { offerId: existingOffer.offerId };`.
fix: If the updateOffer inside the 'already exists' branch fails, propagate the error (or at least return a flag) so the publish path knows the new price was not applied rather than reporting success.
verifier_note: Verified. In createOffer's 'offer entity already exists' recovery, the updateOffer call that re-applies the new pricingSummary is wrapped in try/catch that only logger.warn's on failure (492-499), then unconditionally returns { offerId: existingOffer.offerId } (501) as success. The caller (createProduct line 869-887) then publishes that offer and reports success while the live eBay offer may still carry the old price. There is no signal that the intended price wasn't applied. This is the update branch of an idempotent re-publish, so it's a narrow silent price-integrity gap. Low is the right severity: it only manifests on a re-publish/retry where the offer already exists AND the update sub-call fails, a genuine but uncommon combination.

title: createProduct never pushes inventory to Clover — published items get 0/untracked stock
impact: Seller publishes an item with quantity 25 to their Clover register; the item shows up with 0 stock and is effectively unsellable / mis-counted at the point of sale until a separate inventory sync happens to fire. Silent data loss of the most business-critical field (stock).
evidence: clover.adapter.ts:281-284 returns { platformProductId, platformVariantIds } only, never calls updateCloverItemStock; publish.service.ts:140 `if (platformType === 'ebay')` is the only post-create inventory seeding branch.
fix: After orchestrateCloverProductCreation succeeds, iterate canonicalInventoryLevels and call apiClient.updateCloverItemStock(connection, merchantId, cloverItemId, qty) for each created variant (resolving cloverItemId from platformVariantIds), or have PublishService call adapter.updateInventoryLevels for Clover the same way it seeds eBay. Guard the quantity through safeParseQuantity first.
verifier_note: Verified directly. createProduct (clover.adapter.ts:193-285) builds and pushes only the item group, attributes, options, items and option-associations via orchestrateCloverProductCreation (clover-api-client.service.ts:617-720+) — that orchestrator never calls updateCloverItemStock, so no stock is set on creation. It returns only {platformProductId, platformVariantIds} (line 284); platformInventoryItemIds is undefined, so publish.service.ts:134 defaults it to {}. In publishToConnection, the only post-create inventory seeding is gated on platformType==='ebay' (publish.service.ts:140-156); for Clover there is no follow-up updateInventoryLevels call and no InventoryLevels row is even written for the Clover connection. The base-adapter contract docstring (base-adapter.interface.ts:52) explicitly says createProduct 'Creates a new product with its variants and inventory levels on the platform', which Clover violates. The cross-platform pusher _executeInventoryUpdatePush (sync-coordinator.service.ts:2242+) CAN later ensure a level and push to Clover, but only when a subsequent inventory-change event fires with a defined newQuantity; nothing emits that at publish time, so the freshly published Clover item sits at 0/untracked until an unrelated inventory event happens. Real data-correctness harm to the most business-critical field (POS stock). High retained; recoverability on a later event is the only thing keeping it from critical.

title: ITEM_STOCK webhook computes delta against a wrong-location inventory row, corrupting canonical quantity
impact: Real-time stock webhooks from the Clover register desync canonical inventory: the absolute quantity Clover reports is converted into a delta against the wrong baseline and written to the wrong location bucket, so the canonical total drifts from the POS truth. This then propagates to every other connected channel via emitInventorySyncEvent.
evidence: clover.adapter.ts:827 `locationId = payload?.data?.object?.location?.id || 'default'`; lines 835-840 find currentLevel by connection only; clover.mapper.ts:218 stores PlatformLocationId: primaryCloverLocationId (merchant id). The three location identifiers (merchant id at import, webhook location id or 'default' at webhook) are not guaranteed equal.
fix: Find previousQuantity by matching BOTH PlatformConnectionId AND PlatformLocationId === locationId, and normalize the location id to one canonical value across import and webhook (e.g. always use merchantId, or always use the Clover location id from the payload). Alternatively use applyAbsolute (the absolute peer of applyDelta) since the webhook carries an absolute stock count, avoiding the read-then-delta race entirely.
verifier_note: Verified end to end. In ITEM_STOCK handling, locationId = payload?.data?.object?.location?.id || 'default' (line 827). previousQuantity is read by matching ONLY on PlatformConnectionId (lines 836-838: currentLevels.find(level => level.PlatformConnectionId === connection.Id)), ignoring location. The delta = absolute newQuantity - previousQuantity is then written via inventoryLedger.applyDelta keyed on (variantId, connectionId, locationId) — confirmed in inventory-ledger.service.ts:46-64, which passes p_location_id to rpc_apply_inventory_delta that updates the InventoryLevels projection per (variant, connection, location). Import stores the level under PlatformLocationId = primaryCloverLocationId = cloverData.locations[0].id (clover.mapper.ts:71,218), i.e. the merchant/location id, never 'default'. Clover item_stocks webhooks are merchant-level and carry no nested location, so locationId resolves to 'default' essentially always — meaning the baseline is read from the merchant-id row while the absolute-to-delta result is written to a phantom 'default' row. The merchant-id row is left stale and the 'default' row gets a delta computed against an unrelated baseline; canonical total drifts and is then propagated to all channels via emitInventorySyncEvent (lines 858-867). No test covers this path (only clover.mapper.spec.ts exists; no ITEM_STOCK/applyDelta coverage). The finder's mechanism is correct; the mismatch is effectively guaranteed, not merely possible. High confirmed.

title: PlatformProductId stored prefixed by import but raw by push — only one webhook branch compensates
impact: When a product was created by one path and later acted on by another (or the prefix assumption is wrong for that record), mapping lookups silently return empty: updateProduct treats every existing variant as 'new' (creating duplicate Clover items) and the delete-removed-variants pass finds nothing to delete (orphaned items left on the POS). Webhook ITEM/ITEM_GROUP UPDATE may also fail to locate the product.
evidence: clover.adapter.ts:801-804 dual-lookup workaround proves the inconsistency is known; line 231 `const platformProductId = cloverResponse.itemGroupId;` (raw); clover.mapper.ts:119 `canonicalProductId = clover-prod-${cloverItem.itemGroup.id}` (prefixed); line 383 queries with the raw/unstripped existingMapping.PlatformProductId.
fix: Pick one canonical storage format for PlatformProductId (recommend raw Clover id) and normalize at every write site, or add a shared stripCloverPrefix helper and apply it before every getMappingsByPlatformProductId/getMappingByPlatformId call as the DELETE branch already does.
verifier_note: The underlying inconsistency is real and verified: createProduct pre-creates mappings with platformProductId = cloverResponse.itemGroupId RAW (line 231/268), while the import/webhook mapper stores PlatformProductId = clover-prod-<id> (clover.mapper.ts:119; mappings at clover.adapter.ts:1107). Both getMappingByPlatformId (mappings.service:191-207) and getMappingsByPlatformProductId (588-604) do exact .eq('PlatformProductId', ...) matches. The DELETE webhook tries BOTH objectId and clover-prod-${objectId} (801-804), proving the team knows the formats diverge. HOWEVER the finder's PRIMARY evidence — updateProduct line 383 calling getMappingsByPlatformProductId(connection.Id, results.platformProductId) causing every variant to be treated as 'new' and the orphan-deletion loop to find nothing — is a MISREAD of control flow: results.platformProductId === existingMapping.PlatformProductId (line 300), the verbatim stored value, and PublishService obtains existingMapping by canonical productId+connection (publish.service.ts:93) format-agnostically, so the re-query at 383 returns the same record set it came from; it is self-consistent and does not mis-fire. The REAL harmful path is the ITEM/ITEM_GROUP UPDATE webhook: syncSingleProductFromPlatform re-runs the mapper so cProduct.Id becomes clover-prod-<id> and getMappingByPlatformId(connection.Id, cProduct.Id) at line 1042 MISSES for a push-created (raw-stored) product, creating a duplicate canonical product + mapping. So the finding's impact is real but via line 1042, not line 383. Confirmed at medium; corrected mechanism/location noted.
corrected_location: /Users/dosagie/Documents/CodeProjects/sssync-bknd/src/platform-adapters/clover/clover.adapter.ts:231 (raw push store) vs clover.mapper.ts:119 (prefixed import store); real harm at clover.adapter.ts:1042 (getMappingByPlatformId with prefixed cProduct.Id in syncSingleProductFromPlatform) and the DELETE dual-lookup workaround at clover.adapter.ts:801-804

title: Clover access token is never refreshed and 401 reauth signaling is inconsistent across methods
impact: When a Clover token expires, inventory/product pushes fail as opaque 500s and webhook-driven syncs silently do nothing, with no reliable 'NeedsReauth' flag set outside the publish path. The merchant sees stale data with no prompt to reconnect.
evidence: clover-api-client.service.ts:252-283 (no refresh), lines 325-336 (401 handled only in _fetchWithPagination), updateCloverItemStock catch at 861-865 rethrows InternalServerErrorException for any status; clover.adapter.ts:1143-1145 webhook sync swallows errors without reauth signaling.
fix: Centralize 401 handling in the axios response interceptor (emit connection.auth.error + throw UnauthorizedException for every endpoint, not just pagination); implement Clover refresh-token exchange in getHeaders when the stored token is near/at expiry; ensure webhook-path failures set NeedsReauth when the cause is a 401.

The reauth signal (eventEmitter.emit('connection.auth.error') + UnauthorizedException) is emitted ONLY inside _fetchWithPagination's 401 branch (325-334). updateCloverItemStock's catch rethrows InternalServerErrorException for any failure including 401 (861-865), and syncSingleProductFromPlatform's catch logs and explicitly does NOT re-throw (clover.adapter.ts:1141-1145, comment 'Do not re-throw'), so a token expiry during a webhook-driven sync is silently swallowed with no reauth flag. title: updateProduct converts price with raw multiplication instead of priceToCents
impact: On the product-update path only, a malformed price string can wipe or corrupt the item price on the Clover register instead of being safely defaulted; inconsistent with the create path which is guarded.

Math.round(cVariant.Cost * 100) : undefined (line 425), bypassing priceToCents (translation-utils, used in clover.mapper.ts:273,449,458) which routes through safeParsePrice to strip currency/commas and reject non-finite values. This is a genuine consistency/defensiveness gap worth a trivial fix. title: updateProduct reads v.Quantity off variant objects that have no Quantity; every update pushes 'out of stock'
impact: Any inventory or detail update to a Facebook-listed product marks it out of stock on Facebook, delisting it from Marketplace/Shops even though the seller has stock. This is a direct revenue-loss data-integrity failure on the most common write path.
evidence: Line 475: `availability: v.Quantity > 0 ? 'in stock' : 'out of stock',` with `canonicalVariants.map(v => ...)` (line 469) where v is CanonicalProductVariant (no Quantity). canonicalInventoryLevels param (line 455) is unused. Mirror at line 1321 in updateMarketplaceItems.

Build a Map<variantId, qty> from canonicalInventoryLevels and use that for availability. Same for lat/long — read from canonicalProduct.Metadata.pickupLocation as createProduct already does (lines 383-390), not v.Latitude.
verifier_note: Verified directly. CanonicalProductVariant (canonical-types.ts:43-66 and the adapter-local interface lines 29-43) has NO Quantity/Latitude/Longitude/DeliveryMethod fields. I traced the real caller: sync-coordinator.service.ts:905-973 builds canonicalVariantsForAdapter and never sets Quantity/Latitude/Longitude/DeliveryMethod, and the 5th arg (inventory levels) is passed separately and never read by updateProduct. So at line 475 `v.Quantity` is always undefined, `undefined > 0` is false, and EVERY updateProduct push sets availability:'out of stock'. Latitude/longitude/delivery_method on 476-478 are also always undefined. updateProduct IS reachable (sync-coordinator.service.ts:1048 and :2075). This delists in-stock items on any product-detail update — a real data-integrity/revenue bug. Downgraded critical->high: (a) the dedicated inventory write path updateInventoryLevels (line 582) reads u.level.Quantity correctly, so pure inventory syncs are NOT affected — only the combined updateProduct path is; (b) the cited mirror in updateMarketplaceItems (1321) is genuinely dead code (no callers anywhere in src), so half the cited blast radius is unreachable. The createProduct path is unaffected (hardcodes availability:'in stock' at line 410 and reads location from canonicalProduct.Metadata, not v.Latitude). Still a real, reachable correctness defect.

title: updateProduct swallows all errors and returns {ok:true} — fake success to the sync engine
impact: Failed Facebook updates are reported as successful. Combined with FB-01 (which already corrupts availability), the engine has no signal that anything went wrong, so retries/alerts never fire and data drift is permanent.
evidence: Lines 481-483: `const body = ...; await fetch(...).catch(() => { }); return { ok: true };` No resp.ok check, no error propagation.
fix: Await the fetch, check resp.ok, on failure read resp.text() and return {ok:false, error} or throw, matching updateInventoryLevels' structured-result style (lines 588-603).
verifier_note: Verified verbatim: line 482 `await fetch(...).catch(() => { });` swallows network/throw errors and line 483 unconditionally `return { ok: true };` with no resp.ok check and no body inspection. The caller (sync-coordinator.service.ts:1045-1048) wraps updateProduct in executeWithRetry/circuitBreaker and inspects the result for errors, but a hardcoded {ok:true} gives it nothing to detect, so a failed FB push is recorded as synced and never retried/alerted. Real fake-success defect on the reachable updateProduct path. High (not critical): it is a masking/observability failure rather than direct data destruction on its own; its main harm is compounding FB-01.

title: createProduct returns empty-but-success object on failure instead of throwing; items_batch async result discarded
impact: The sync engine persists a platform mapping with empty or unverified IDs, believing the product is live on Facebook when it is not. Subsequent updates/deletes target a non-existent id. Errors are invisible.
evidence: Lines 334, 342, 445 return `{ platformProductId: '', platformVariantIds: {} }`. Lines 447-452: response parsed but handle ignored; `canonicalVariants.forEach(v => { map[v.Id] = v.Sku || ... })` returns SKU mapping regardless of batch outcome.
fix: Throw on decrypt/token/catalog failure and on !resp.ok so the engine records a real failure. Capture the returned handle and (optionally) poll check_batch_request_status to confirm creation before writing the mapping.
verifier_note: Verified: createProduct returns {platformProductId:'', platformVariantIds:{}} on decrypt failure (334), missing token/catalog (342), and non-2xx items_batch (445), contradicting the BaseAdapter contract ('...or throws an error', base-adapter.interface.ts:56-64). On success (447-452) it discards the items_batch handle and maps v.Id->SKU without confirming the async batch created the item — correct observation that items_batch 200 is acceptance, not creation. createProduct IS reachable (sync-coordinator.service.ts:722/1679, publish.service.ts:125, products.service.ts multiple). However I downgrade high->medium: empty-string platformProductId/variantId mappings are detectable downstream (a caller persisting '' as an id is conspicuous, and the empty-id returns occur on guard paths that already logged warnings/errors), and the unverified-SKU mapping is inherent to FB's async catalog model (there is a separate check_batch_request_status method). The real defect is contract violation + no post-batch verification, which is a correctness/robustness gap rather than guaranteed data loss on every call.

title: updateInventoryLevels reports whole batch as success on HTTP 200, swallowing per-item items_batch failures
impact: Partial inventory-push failures are hidden from the sync engine; a variant whose update FB rejected is marked synced. Multi-variant atomicity is also absent (no per-item status), so the engine cannot retry just the failed ones.
evidence: Lines 588-598: on `response.ok` returns `successCount: requests.length` with no inspection of the items_batch validation_status / errors array in the body. Availability-only mapping at line 582.
fix: Parse the items_batch response and, where available, the handle's check_batch_request_status, and report per-request success/failure. At minimum read response JSON and downgrade successCount for items with errors.
verifier_note: Verified: on response.ok the method returns successCount: requests.length (line 598) without parsing the items_batch body for per-item validation_status/errors. FB items_batch is asynchronous and partial-failure-capable, so per-item rejections (bad id/value) are reported as successes — accurate. It also maps quantity to binary in/out-of-stock (line 582), losing real counts, and never resolves a PlatformLocationId. updateInventoryLevels IS reachable (sync-coordinator.service.ts:2520, transactional-sync.service.ts:148, cross-account-sync.service.ts:305). Downgraded high->medium: FB's catalog model genuinely has no per-location numeric stock (the binary-availability mapping is a documented platform limitation, not a bug), and the success-on-200 over-reporting is an observability/partial-retry gap rather than direct corruption — the HTTP-level failures (the common case: bad token, 4xx/5xx) ARE handled correctly (lines 592-596). The uncovered gap is only the rarer in-batch per-item rejection.

title: Inventory webhook collapses real stock to binary 1/0, corrupting canonical quantity via ledger delta
impact: A benign Facebook availability webhook can silently overwrite a seller's true multi-unit stock down to 1 and fan that out to Shopify/Square/eBay, causing real oversell/undersell. Genuine data corruption.
evidence: Line 788: `const newQuantity = availability === 'in stock' || availability === 'available' ? 1 : 0;` then lines 793-807 apply `delta = newQuantity - previousQuantity` via inventoryLedger.applyDelta and emit cross-platform sync (line 812).
fix: Treat FB availability as a delist/relist signal, not an absolute count: only force qty to 0 on out-of-stock, and on in-stock either leave canonical quantity untouched or restore from a stored last-known value — never hard-set to 1. Do not emit a destructive delta from a binary availability flag.
verifier_note: Verified the mechanism end to end. handleInventoryChanges:788 collapses availability to newQuantity = 1|0, then emitInventorySyncEvent:812 emits newQuantity (the binary 1). I traced the consumer: sync-coordinator pushes this newQuantity into the shared pool via inventoryService.updatePoolInventoryLevel (2310-2328), which then propagates to other platforms — so a benign FB in-stock webhook can overwrite a multi-unit canonical/pool quantity down to 1 and fan it to Shopify/Square/eBay. That is the real oversell/data-corruption harm. One correction to the finder's precise mechanism: the cited ledger delta (line 794-807) is applied to the FACEBOOK connection's OWN level, and since FB import always writes that level as 1/0 (mapper :214/:250, save at :973), previousQuantity is already 1 when in-stock, so delta=0 and that specific ledger write is usually a no-op. The actual damage vector is the emitted newQuantity:1 overwriting OTHER platforms/the pool, not the FB-connection ledger drop the finder emphasized. Net harm is real and reachable (catalog webhook field 'inventory'/'availability', line 662), so confirmed; severity high not critical because it requires a pool/multi-platform setup and an availability-only webhook.

title: Version skew between getApiClient (v24.0), createProduct (v20.0) and updateInventoryLevels (v20.0)
impact: When Facebook sunsets v20.0 (older versions are deprecated on a rolling ~2yr basis), create and inventory pushes start failing while reads keep working, and the swallow-and-fake-success behavior hides it. Hard-to-diagnose, version-time-bomb failure.
evidence: Line 88 `const version = 'v24.0';`; line 339 `const version = 'v20.0'; // Updated to match getApiClient version` (incorrect comment); line 563 `const version = 'v20.0';`.
fix: Centralize the Graph version in one constant (or config) and use it everywhere; align all write paths to the same supported version as reads.
verifier_note: Verified the version strings: getApiClient='v24.0' (88), createProduct='v20.0' with the demonstrably false comment '// Updated to match getApiClient version' (339), updateInventoryLevels='v20.0' (563), syncSingleProductFromPlatform='v20.0' (1061), while update/delete/marketplace/order/messaging mix v24.0 and v20.0. So the version skew and the wrong comment are factually present. Downgraded medium->low: this is a latent maintenance/time-bomb, not a present-tense correctness or money bug — v20.0 still functions today, and the 'swallowed when sunset' impact is contingent on FB-03/04/05 and a future deprecation date. It is a real but low-urgency hygiene issue.

title: syncFromPlatform has no rate-limit handling and silently aborts pagination on any non-OK page
impact: On rate-limited or transiently failing large catalogs, only a partial product set is imported and the rest is silently dropped, with the sync logged as 'complete'. Items beyond the failure point appear deleted/missing in canonical data.
evidence: Lines 119-124: `const res = await fetch(nextUrl, { headers }); if (!res.ok) break; ... nextUrl = data?.paging?.next || '';` with no status inspection or retry. Same pattern lines 149-160. syncFromPlatform logs 'Sync complete' regardless (line 324).
fix: On 429/5xx honor Retry-After with bounded exponential backoff and retry the same page; on persistent failure throw rather than break, so a partial sync is not reported as complete.
verifier_note: Verified: both pagination loops do `if (!res.ok) break;` (lines 120 and 151) with no status inspection, no Retry-After/backoff, no retry — any single non-200 page (incl. 429 throttle) silently truncates the result. syncFromPlatform (293) then iterates only the items fetched and logs 'Sync complete: N success' (324) regardless, treating a truncated catalog as complete. On large/rate-limited catalogs this silently drops items, and because canonical reconciliation can interpret missing items as deletions, truncation can cascade. Accurate and reachable. Severity confirmed medium: requires a rate-limit/transient-failure condition to manifest, and FB returns 200 with empty-page paging in the normal case, so it is a robustness gap that bites under load rather than a guaranteed every-run failure.

title: PlatformProductId stored prefixed on sync but raw on push — breaks dedupe, creates duplicate products and re-pushed listings
impact: A product that was published via Anorha (push) gets a mapping with PlatformProductId='123'. When syncFromPlatform later pulls that same product back, it looks up getMappingByPlatformId(conn, 'whatnot-prod-123'), finds nothing, decides no Supabase product exists, and calls productsService.saveProduct(...) creating a DUPLICATE canonical product (adapter.ts:116-122). Variant mappings are then re-created. The seller ends up with two canonical products for one real Whatnot product, and subsequent inventory/price syncs can diverge across the duplicates.
evidence: adapter.ts:162 `PlatformProductId: cProduct.Id!` (prefixed) vs adapter.ts:221 `const platformProductId = created.id;` (raw) passed to createMappingWithIdentifiers at :259. Lookup adapter.ts:108 `getMappingByPlatformId(connection.Id, cProduct.Id!)` with cProduct.Id prefixed (mapper.ts:53). getMappingByPlatformId uses exact equality (mappings.service.ts:197).
fix: Pick ONE canonical storage format for PlatformProductId and use it on every write/read. Easiest: store the RAW Whatnot id everywhere (strip the `whatnot-prod-` prefix in persistWhatnotProducts before writing the mapping, and look it up with the stripped id), matching what the push path and the rest of the adapter's `.replace('whatnot-prod-','')` calls already assume.
verifier_note: Verified the format mismatch directly. Sync path: mapper.ts:53 builds cProduct.Id = `whatnot-prod-${id}`; persistWhatnotProducts writes PlatformProductId: cProduct.Id! (adapter.ts:162, prefixed) and dedup-looks-up getMappingByPlatformId(conn, cProduct.Id!) with exact .eq (service:191-199, prefixed). Push path: createProduct sets platformProductId = created.id (RAW, adapter.ts:221) and both its own pre-created mapping (adapter.ts:259) and PublishService.createMappingsForVariants (publish.service.ts:137->327->createMappingWithIdentifiers) store that RAW id as PlatformProductId (service:155, unmodified). So a push-created mapping has PlatformProductId='123' while sync's dedup queries 'whatnot-prod-123' and finds nothing -> savedSupabaseProduct stays null -> saveProduct creates a DUPLICATE canonical product (adapter.ts:116-122). Note updateProduct/deleteProduct strip the prefix defensively (replace('whatnot-prod-','')) confirming the codebase's own expectation that the stored value is prefixed; the push path violates that invariant. Reachable in normal operation (publish then later sync). Real duplicate-product / inventory-divergence harm. High confirmed.
corrected_location: whatnot.adapter.ts:108 (lookup, prefixed) vs :221/:259 + publish.service.ts:137/:327 (push stores raw); persist via platform-product-mappings.service.ts:155

title: updateProduct reports a variant as successfully updated when its listing id cannot be resolved and the price write is silently skipped
impact: A price change a user made in Anorha is silently dropped on Whatnot while the sync engine is told the variant updated successfully. No error surfaces, no retry is triggered, and the listing keeps its old price. This is silent data loss of a money-bearing field.
evidence: adapter.ts:327-336: `if (mapping?.PlatformVariantId) { const listingId = await this.resolveListingId(...); if (listingId) { await apiClient.updateListing(... price ...); } results.updatedVariantIds.push(mapping.PlatformVariantId); }` — the push to updatedVariantIds is outside the `if (listingId)` block. resolveListingId can legitimately return null (adapter.ts:471-477).

In that case the price is silently dropped yet reported as updated with no error. Genuinely harmful (silent loss of a money field, no retry signal). Downgraded high->medium because both push-created and sync-created mappings normally carry whatnotListingId in PlatformSpecificData (adapter.ts:265, mapper.ts:93->Metadata->service:161), so the null-listingId path is the exception (variant whose listing was deleted/never created), not the common case.
